event: Early hunter-gatherer communities inhabit the region, leaving evidence such as the Iwo Eleru rock shelter skeleton in present-day southwestern Nigeria.
The Nok culture emerges on the Jos Plateau, producing distinctive terracotta sculptures and developing early ironworking in West Africa.
The Nok people practice iron smelting, making the region one of the earliest sites of ironworking south of the Sahara.
The Kanem Empire rises around Lake Chad, growing into a powerful trans-Saharan trading state in the northeast.
The city of Igbo-Ukwu flourishes, leaving sophisticated bronze artifacts that reveal advanced metalworking and long-distance trade.
The Yoruba city-state of Ife rises as a major religious and artistic center, later renowned for its naturalistic bronze and terracotta heads.
King Humai of Kanem converts to Islam, beginning the spread of the faith across the northeastern states.
The Hausa city-states, including Kano, Katsina, and Zaria, develop as commercial and craft centers across the northern savanna.
Oduduwa traditions place the founding of the Yoruba kingdom of Ife and the dynasties that would spread across Yorubaland.
The Kingdom of Benin emerges in the south, ruled by the Oba and famed for its bronze plaques and walled city.
The Kanem-Bornu Empire consolidates power, dominating trans-Saharan trade routes from its base near Lake Chad.
Oba Ewuare the Great expands the Kingdom of Benin and fortifies Benin City with extensive walls and earthworks.
Portuguese navigators reach the coast of Benin, opening direct European trade contact with the region.
Portugal establishes formal trade relations with the Kingdom of Benin, exchanging goods including pepper, ivory, and enslaved people.
Mai Idris Alooma of Kanem-Bornu modernizes his army with firearms and Turkish military advisers, expanding the empire.
The Oyo Empire grows into the dominant Yoruba power, its cavalry controlling trade across the southwest.
The Atlantic slave trade intensifies along the Bight of Benin and Bight of Biafra, devastating coastal and interior communities.
Usman dan Fodio launches a jihad that overthrows the Hausa kings and establishes the Sokoto Caliphate.
The Sokoto Caliphate consolidates control over much of northern Nigeria, becoming one of Africa's largest pre-colonial states.
The Oyo Empire begins to disintegrate amid internal revolts and the Fulani jihad, plunging Yorubaland into prolonged warfare.
Britain bombards and intervenes in Lagos, deposing Oba Kosoko in a move to suppress the slave trade and expand influence.
Britain annexes Lagos as a colony, establishing a permanent imperial foothold on the coast.
The Berlin Conference recognizes British claims over the Niger region during the Scramble for Africa.
The Royal Niger Company receives a royal charter to administer trade and territory along the Niger River.
A British punitive expedition sacks Benin City, ending the Kingdom of Benin and looting thousands of the Benin Bronzes.
Britain establishes the Protectorates of Northern and Southern Nigeria after revoking the Royal Niger Company's charter.
British forces under Frederick Lugard defeat the Sokoto Caliphate, completing the conquest of northern Nigeria.
Britain amalgamates the Northern and Southern Protectorates into the single Colony and Protectorate of Nigeria.
The Aba Women's Riots erupt as Igbo women protest colonial taxation and the warrant chief system.
Nnamdi Azikiwe and Herbert Macaulay found the National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons, energizing the independence movement.
A new federal constitution reorganizes Nigeria into three semi-autonomous regions, paving the way toward self-government.
Nigeria gains independence from Britain on 1 October and joins the United Nations, with Abubakar Tafawa Balewa as prime minister.
Nigeria becomes a federal republic with Nnamdi Azikiwe as its first president.
Two military coups topple the civilian government, leading to the deaths of leaders and ethnic violence that destabilizes the nation.
The Eastern Region secedes as the Republic of Biafra, triggering the Nigerian Civil War.
The Nigerian Civil War ends with Biafra's surrender after roughly a million deaths, mostly from famine.
Nigeria joins OPEC as oil becomes the dominant pillar of its economy.
General Yakubu Gowon is overthrown in a bloodless coup, beginning a series of military regime changes.
Nigeria begins moving its federal capital to the planned, centrally located city of Abuja.
Civilian rule returns as Shehu Shagari is elected president, inaugurating the Second Republic.
General Muhammadu Buhari seizes power in a military coup, ending the troubled Second Republic.
The federal capital officially moves from Lagos to Abuja.
The military annuls the presidential election widely won by Moshood Abiola, sparking a national political crisis under Sani Abacha.
The execution of writer and activist Ken Saro-Wiwa draws international condemnation and Nigeria's suspension from the Commonwealth.
Nigeria returns to democratic civilian rule as Olusegun Obasanjo is elected president, beginning the Fourth Republic.
Boko Haram is founded in the northeast, later launching a violent insurgency against the state.
Boko Haram's uprising escalates into a full-scale insurgency, displacing millions across the northeast over the following years.
Boko Haram abducts 276 schoolgirls in Chibok, prompting the global Bring Back Our Girls campaign.
Muhammadu Buhari wins the presidency in Nigeria's first democratic transfer of power to an opposition candidate.
Mass EndSARS protests against police brutality sweep major cities, culminating in the Lekki tollgate shooting.
Bola Tinubu is inaugurated as president after winning a closely contested election.
